title: Project Capstone Planning
subtitle: Plan, Design, Build — Your GUI Masterpiece!
overview: Students plan and design their capstone GUI project using Python and Tkinter. This session focuses on understanding project requirements, wireframing the interface, and mapping out the logic before writing a single line of code.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — whiteboard wireframes, student sketches, GUI mockups, planning sheets.

keyTopics: What is a GUI? — Graphical User Interface vs Command Line
Tkinter — Python's built-in GUI library (no install needed!)
Core Widgets — Label, Button, Entry, Frame, Canvas, Listbox
Event Handling — bind(), command= and the mainloop()
Wireframing — Sketch the UI before coding
Capstone Project Planning — Title, Purpose, Widgets, Logic, Stretch Goals
MVC Thinking — Separating UI from Logic

activity.title: Plan Your Capstone GUI Project
steps: Choose your project idea — Calculator, To-Do App, Quiz App, Weather App, etc.
Write down: Project Title, Purpose, Target User
Sketch a wireframe — draw the window layout on paper
List all widgets you'll need (buttons, labels, entry fields, etc.)
Write the logic flow — what happens when each button is clicked?
Create a basic Tkinter window with a title and size
Add one widget and connect it to a simple function
Share your plan with the class — peer feedback!

quiz.explanation: GUI = Graphical User Interface! It's the visual part of a program — windows, buttons, text boxes — that users interact with instead of typing commands.

quiz.q: Which Python library is used to create GUI applications without installing anything extra?
quiz.explanation: Tkinter comes built-in with Python! No pip install needed — just import tkinter and start building.

quiz.q: What does mainloop() do in a Tkinter program?
options: Keeps the window open and listens for user events
Loops through a list of widgets
quiz.explanation: mainloop() is the heartbeat of your GUI! It keeps the window alive and continuously listens for clicks, key presses, and other events.

quiz.q: Which widget is used to accept text input from the user in Tkinter?
quiz.explanation: Entry is the text input box widget! Users can type into it, and your code can read the value using .get().

options: Writing Python code for the backend
Sketching the layout of your app before coding
quiz.explanation: Wireframing means drawing a rough sketch of your app's layout — where buttons, labels and fields will go — before writing any code. Plan first, code second!

quiz.q: Which layout manager arranges widgets in a table/grid format?
quiz.explanation: grid() lets you place widgets in rows and columns — like a table. Perfect for forms, calculators, and structured layouts!

quiz.q: How do you set the title of a Tkinter window?
quiz.explanation: window.title('My App') sets the text shown in the title bar of the window. Simple and useful!

quiz.q: What does the command= parameter do in a Button widget?
options: Links the button to a function that runs when clicked
quiz.explanation: command= is how you connect a button to a function! When the user clicks, Tkinter calls that function automatically.

quiz.q: Which of these is a good capstone project idea for a Python GUI app?
options: A To-Do List App with add and delete features
quiz.explanation: A To-Do List App is a perfect beginner capstone! It uses Labels, Entry, Buttons, and a Listbox — covering all major Tkinter concepts.

quiz.q: What is the first step before writing code for any GUI project?
options: Run the code to see what happens
quiz.explanation: Always plan before you code! Wireframe the layout, list your widgets, and think through the logic — it saves a lot of debugging time later.

quiz.q: Which Tkinter widget is used to display text or images (non-editable)?
quiz.explanation: Label displays text or images on screen. It's read-only — the user can see it but not edit it directly.

quiz.q: What does .get() do when used with an Entry widget?
options: Reads and returns the text typed by the user
quiz.explanation: .get() retrieves whatever the user typed into the Entry box. You'll use this constantly — for search bars, forms, calculators!

quiz.q: In MVC thinking, what does the 'V' (View) represent?
options: The visual interface the user sees
quiz.explanation: View = what the user sees — your Tkinter widgets and layout. Keeping the View separate from the Logic (Model) makes your code cleaner and easier to fix.

quiz.q: Which method is used to set the window size in Tkinter?
quiz.explanation: window.geometry('800x600') sets width x height in pixels. You can also set the starting position: '800x600+100+50'.

quiz.q: Why is planning your project before coding important?
options: It helps you avoid errors, save time, and build a clearer app
It is required by Tkinter to work
It is not important — just start coding!
quiz.explanation: Planning saves time! When you know what you're building, which widgets you need, and how the logic flows — you write better code with fewer bugs.
